narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 64% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in academic records, test scores, and background details of past students, focusing on factors like grades, LSAT performance, and study commitments that best signal bar success. The strongest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your plan to attend part-time, as the vast majority of students who passed the bar—over 93%—were full-time, allowing deeper focus without work distractions that often derail preparation. While your third-year decile ranking of 7 worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it exceeds the average for bar passers at about 5.9, it wasn't enough to offset the part-time concern. Your first-year decile of 4, however, nudged things toward failure, sitting below the typical 6 for those who passed and signaling an early academic struggle. Your LSAT score of 37 and undergraduate GPA of 3.20 were close to averages for successful students, offering some balance, while your middle family income bracket aligns with over a third of bar passers. These top factors—part-time status as the biggest red flag, tempered by your later-year improvement—shaped the overall call more than anything else. Compared to admitted applicants, who typically show stronger early grades and full-time dedication, your profile raised valid risks based on historical outcomes. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your solid LSAT and recent academic gains showing real potential. To strengthen a future application, consider committing to full-time study if possible, and focus on boosting early performance through targeted support like tutoring. You might also retake the LSAT or gather letters highlighting your resilience and upward trajectory. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the prediction in your favor—many students turn things around with strategic changes. Keep pushing; your third-year progress proves you have what it takes.
